question_text: What is the main supervision value of clear written shift assignments?
answer_a: They help staff understand duties, coverage priorities, and who to notify when a barrier appears.
answer_b: They eliminate the need for administrator follow-up.
answer_c: They allow staff to ignore training records when shifts are busy.
answer_d: They prove the facility has regulatory approval for the course.
rationale: Clear written assignments support role clarity and communication; they do not replace follow-up, training verification, or approval metadata.

question_text: Which training-record element most helps an administrator connect staff readiness to assigned duties?
answer_a: A general note that the staff member was trained sometime in the past.
answer_b: A dated topic entry tied to the staff role, method of instruction, and any follow-up required.
answer_c: A verbal reminder with no record because the task is routine.
answer_d: A copy of an unrelated course flyer.
rationale: A dated role-related training entry gives the administrator a traceable basis for assigning duties and planning follow-up.

question_text: What should an administrator review when the same shift duty is repeatedly missed?
answer_a: Only whether the caregiver intended to miss the task.
answer_b: Whether the task can be removed from all records.
answer_c: Role clarity, assignment method, training readiness, staffing pattern, and follow-up controls.
answer_d: Whether the issue can wait until the next licensing visit.
rationale: Repeated missed duties may signal a supervision-system gap, so the review should include assignments, training, staffing, and follow-up.

question_text: Which statement best describes administrator accountability for staff supervision?
answer_a: The administrator is accountable only when personally present during a shift.
answer_b: Accountability ends once staff receive orientation.
answer_c: Informal reminders are enough if staff are experienced.
answer_d: The administrator sets expectations, monitors follow-through, corrects gaps, and keeps records that support continuity.
rationale: Administrator accountability includes active oversight and documentation, not merely personal presence or one-time orientation.

question_text: A fictional evening shift misses dining-room coverage because two caregivers thought the other person was assigned. What response most directly corrects the supervision gap?
answer_a: Clarify coverage immediately, review the assignment tool, confirm related training, and document a follow-up check.
answer_b: Tell both caregivers that they should have known better without changing the assignment process.
answer_c: Delete the shift note because no real resident name was used.
answer_d: Wait to address the issue until it happens a third time.
rationale: The scenario points to unclear role assignment, so the administrator should correct the immediate gap and strengthen the assignment and training controls.

question_text: A fictional caregiver says a handoff checklist is not important because care tasks were completed. What is the best coaching response?
answer_a: Stop using the checklist for that caregiver only.
answer_b: Privately explain the checklist purpose, review the expectation, identify barriers, document coaching, and set follow-up.
answer_c: Post a warning about the caregiver on a staff bulletin board.
answer_d: Ignore the comment because direct care matters more than records.
rationale: Private, objective coaching connects the checklist to shift communication and creates a fair follow-up record.

question_text: A fictional shift lead gives verbal assignments but never updates the written assignment sheet. What should the administrator do first in the supervision review?
answer_a: Assume verbal direction is enough if staff seem busy.
answer_b: Remove assignment sheets from the workflow.
answer_c: Compare verbal practice with the written process, clarify the expected tool, and document the correction.
answer_d: Tell staff to vote on which assignment method they prefer for the day.
rationale: A mismatch between verbal practice and written tools creates role confusion; the administrator should align the process and record the correction.

question_text: During a fictional record audit, a staff member is assigned a task but has no role-specific training entry for that duty. Which administrator action is most appropriate?
answer_a: Assume prior experience is enough and keep assigning the task without review.
answer_b: Ask the staff member to create a backdated record.
answer_c: Ignore the gap because the duty is common.
answer_d: Verify training needs, provide or document appropriate training, and review whether the assignment should continue during the gap.
rationale: The administrator should connect assignments to documented readiness and avoid backdating or ignoring training-record gaps.

question_text: A fictional caregiver misses the same documentation step twice after reminders. Which coaching record is strongest?
answer_a: An objective note stating the observed misses, expectation reviewed, support offered, staff response if relevant, and follow-up date.
answer_b: A note saying the caregiver has a bad attitude.
answer_c: No note because reminders were already given.
answer_d: A promise that the problem will never occur again.
rationale: A strong coaching record uses objective facts, the expected standard, support, and follow-up rather than opinions or guarantees.

question_text: A fictional staffing pattern leaves one role unclear every weekend. What should the administrator avoid?
answer_a: Reviewing weekend assignments for recurring gaps.
answer_b: Treating each event as unrelated without reviewing the staffing pattern or assignment process.
answer_c: Checking whether training and handoff tools support the weekend duties.
answer_d: Setting a follow-up audit after clarifying the role.
rationale: Recurring weekend ambiguity suggests a pattern; treating each event as isolated prevents correction of the supervision system.

question_text: A fictional supervisor wants to correct a caregiver in front of coworkers to make an example. What is the better administrator direction?
answer_a: Proceed publicly so no documentation is needed.
answer_b: Skip coaching because coworkers already know the issue.
answer_c: Use private, respectful coaching with objective expectations and a documented follow-up plan.
answer_d: Ask coworkers to decide the consequence.
rationale: Private and objective coaching protects dignity while still documenting expectations and follow-up.

question_text: A fictional shift note reports a missed task but does not say what was corrected or who was notified. What should the administrator do?
answer_a: Accept the note because any documentation is enough.
answer_b: Discard the note and rely on memory.
answer_c: Wait until the next staff meeting to ask general questions only.
answer_d: Clarify objective facts, action taken, notification or escalation, and follow-up needed.
rationale: A defensible supervision record should show what happened, what was done, who was notified when needed, and what follow-up remains.

question_text: Which action best demonstrates judgment when a staff performance issue may affect resident safety?
answer_a: Move beyond routine coaching as needed by using the approved escalation pathway and documenting the reason.
answer_b: Keep the issue informal to avoid creating a record.
answer_c: Delay review until the staff member asks for help.
answer_d: Treat all performance issues as identical regardless of risk.
rationale: Potential resident-safety impact changes the response; escalation and documentation help the administrator manage risk appropriately.

question_text: What is the best reason to set a follow-up date after coaching?
answer_a: It lets the administrator close the matter without checking results.
answer_b: It creates a planned point to verify whether the expected practice changed or whether further action is needed.
answer_c: It replaces the training record.
answer_d: It prevents staff from asking questions.
rationale: A follow-up date closes the supervision loop by testing whether coaching changed practice or whether additional response is required.

question_text: Which documentation wording is most appropriate for a coaching note?
answer_a: The caregiver was careless again.
answer_b: Everyone knows this employee is difficult.
answer_c: On 06/01 in the fictional scenario, the checklist was not completed by end of shift; expectation reviewed; follow-up check set for next scheduled shift.
answer_d: This will never happen again.
rationale: Objective wording records observable facts, the expectation reviewed, and follow-up without blame or unsupported promises.

question_text: When should an administrator consider whether routine coaching is not enough?
answer_a: Only after a regulator requests the file.
answer_b: Only when the staff member agrees in writing.
answer_c: Never, because coaching is always sufficient.
answer_d: When the concern suggests risk to resident safety, rights, required reporting, staffing coverage, or ongoing compliance.
rationale: Higher-risk concerns require review for escalation rather than automatic reliance on routine coaching.

question_text: What Moodle evidence should be retained for the Hour 1 role-clarity interaction?
answer_a: The learner's selected branch, feedback viewed, remediation if needed, submitted documentation elements, and completion timestamp.
answer_b: Only a screenshot of the course homepage.
answer_c: Only the learner's name without response data.
answer_d: No evidence if the learner later passes the quiz.
rationale: Scenario evidence should show the learner completed the decision path and submitted the required documentation elements.

question_text: Which learner submission should be rejected or remediated for the application assignment?
answer_a: A fictional plan identifying a duty, record, coaching message, follow-up point, and escalation threshold.
answer_b: A response that includes real staff names, real facility facts, or identifiable personnel details.
answer_c: A de-identified example using generic dates and fictional roles.
answer_d: A brief objective plan written in complete sentences.
rationale: The course requires fictional, non-identifying submissions; real personnel or facility details violate the privacy boundary.

question_text: Why does the course require both scenario practice and a post-test?
answer_a: Scenario practice replaces the assessment requirement.
answer_b: The post-test replaces participation and assignment evidence.
answer_c: Scenario practice checks applied decision-making, while the post-test confirms knowledge across the course objectives.
answer_d: Both activities exist only for learner satisfaction surveys.
rationale: The two activity types generate different evidence: applied judgment in scenarios and broader knowledge assessment in the quiz.

question_text: What must remain true before any certificate release for this pending course?
answer_a: A learner may receive a certificate after viewing the overview only.
answer_b: A quiz score alone is enough for certificate release.
answer_c: The instructor may bypass missing approval metadata for experienced learners.
answer_d: Required duration, activity evidence, post-test pass, evaluation, and approval metadata gates must be satisfied.
rationale: Certificate release is blocked until completion and approval-related gates are met; a quiz score alone is not sufficient.
